desc:
The road is very well kept here, with grey stones fitting tight together. The
slow murmur of the wind blowing through the high hedge can be heard below sounds
of birds singing in some deciduous trees north of the road. The hedge is thin
and dead in many places, even burnt. Nowhere can you find a way through it
though, and it is still impassable.
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most frequented road of all Middle-earth. You can walk
without any difficulties on this nice and smooth road. But be sure not to get
off this road if you want no trouble.
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most frequented road of all Middle-earth. You can walk
without any difficulties on this nice and smooth road. But be sure not to get
off this road if you want no trouble.
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most popular road of Middle-earth. There seems to be no
real difficulty to walk on this well prepared road. You feel quite safe on this
road, but you shiver as you look around you. You wish you'd never have to quit
this road...the outside world is so frightening !
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most popular road of Middle-earth. There seems to be no
real difficulty to walk on this well prepared road. You feel quite safe on this
road, but you shiver as you look around you. You wish you'd never have to quit
this road...the outside world is so frightening !
name: Old East Road

desc:
You are standing on the most popular road of Middle-earth. There seems to be no
real difficulty to walk on this well prepared road. You feel quite safe on this
road, but you shiver as you look around you. You wish you'd never have to quit
this road...the outside world is so frightening !
name: Old East Road

desc:
This is the great road which stretches east and west across Eriador. Built over
two thousand years ago, the traffic along this road must have once been
considerable to justify the expense. Nowadays, there are few travellers to
marvel at this great feat of construction. The road cuts through the flat land
here, shadowed by the impressive hedge of thorny bushes to the south and the
thick expanse of bracken of the plains to the north.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
The road here curves northward to approach closer under the watchful eye of
Weathertop. This great conical hill lies fully ten miles in a northwesterly
direction, yet from this distance it is still striking.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
The road bends here slightly, the westerly stretch snaking southwards before
continuing onwards to Bree. East, the road passes within a few miles of the
southern flank of Weathertop, known as Amon Sul by the learned.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
This is a lonely countryside, flat for miles about, bar the north where lies the
distinctive shape of Weathertop. Isolated as it is from the rest of the range of
hills, it marks the southern end of the Emyn Sul. A trail in the flat terrain
before the hill curves southwards, towards this road.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
Directly south of Weathertop, a meeting of ways occurs. The first is the great
road built by Elendil's followers long ago, unbroken for many miles in either
the east or west direction. The second route is a simple trail, with few stones
to mark it. This trail crosses the passage of the road.
name: Crossing of the Paths

desc:
You are standing on a small path, hills lying to the north. You almost can't see
where the path leads to; this path doesn't seem to be used very often.
name: The Small Trail

desc:
You are standing on a small path, hills lying to the north. You almost can't see
where the path leads to; this path doesn't seem to be used very often.
name: The Small Trail

desc:
This small trail winds its way amongst the hills which rise above you to the
north of here. As it twists and turns through the rolling countryside, it is
often quite impossible to make out where the path might lead, and it is perhaps
for this reason that it is so seldom used. Thick weeds and long grasses grow
among the stones of the trail, almost obscuring it from view in places.
name: The Small Trail

desc:
You are standing on a small path, surrounding the hills to the north. You almost
can't see where the path leads to; this path doesn't seem to be used very often.
name: The Small Trail

desc:
You are standing on a small path, surrounding the hills to the north. You almost
can't see where the path leads to; this path doesn't seem to be used very often.
name: The Small Trail

desc:
You are standing on a small path, surrounding the hills to the north. You almost
can't see where the path leads to; this path doesn't seem to be used very often.
name: The Small Trail

desc:
The trail seems to have enlarged itself. You can now see through the dense
vegetation. You can sense an unknown force, surrounding you. Its evil aura makes
you shiver.
name: The Small Clearing

desc:
The path seems to become smaller and smaller. You begin to doubt if this path
was the right direction to take. The vegetation obscures the passage; you
scratch yourself everywhere. What a nasty region ! You swear loudly; it was
maybe a word too much : you have woken up all the strange animals hiding in the
bushes...
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
The path seems to become smaller and smaller. You begin to doubt if this path
was the right direction to take. The vegetation obscures the passage; you
scratch yourself everywhere. What a nasty region ! You swear loudly; it was
maybe a word too much : you have woken up all the strange animals hiding in the
bushes...
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
The path seems to become smaller and smaller. You begin to doubt if this path
was the right direction to take. The vegetation obscures the passage; you
scratch yourself everywhere. What a nasty region ! You swear loudly; it was
maybe a word too much : you have woken up all the strange animals hiding in the
bushes...
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
The path seems to become smaller and smaller. You begin to doubt if this path
was the right direction to take. The vegetation obscures the passage; you
scratch yourself everywhere. What a nasty region ! You swear loudly; it was
maybe a word too much : you have woken up all the strange animals hiding in the
bushes...
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
The trail continues, hard to distinguish among the grasses and small bushes that
grow here. The land is flat here. Only the hills in the northern and eastern
distances break the flat lands stand out from the plain.
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
You find burrs sticking to you as you follow the trail through the grasses. You
hear the sounds of insects and birds about you, but there is no sign of other
people... even the trail might have been made by animals. Far off in the east,
the rounded green shapes of the Weather Hills are the only sign that this plain
has an end.
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
Someone made a trail here among the barren plains. It is hard to pick out among
the rocks and the short grass, but it goes west and south bypassing a small,
seemingly deserted village to the north. From the north to the southeast a line
of mighty hills dominates the horizon.
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
Someone has made a trail here among the treeless plains. It is hard to pick out
among the short grass and the wildflowers, but it leads to the north and to the
east, avoiding the walled town northeast of you. From the north to the southeast
a line of hills dominates the horizon.
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
Someone has made a trail here among the barren plains. It is hard to pick out
among the rocks and the short grass, but it leads to the west and to the south,
purposely avoiding the walled town to your east. No smoke comes from that town,
nor are the plains about it cultivated. From the north to the southeast a line
of mighty hills dominates the horizon.
name: A Tiny Trail

desc:
Grass grows on the trail, making it hard to distinguish from the grasslands all
about. There are almost no trees here, and if people once grew crops or herded
cattle on the plains, there are no longer any signs of it. Birds, beasts, and
bugs are the only dwellers here.
name: Trail through the Plains

desc:
Hiking along this trail is quite quaint and lovely, there are few stubs of tree
roots that make this path a little bumpy but not at all harsh. Parts of the
trail look forgotten and deserted, while others look well kept. Most likely this
is just from the natural abandonment of the area and not because of anything
else. The lands around the trail seems to change quite often and there looks to
be no real cabins around here, a few dead trails suggest that maybe there once
were people living around here, but they are all gone now.
name: A Trail in the Woods

desc:
Cutting a thin track through the bracken-carpeted plains is this trail that
connects the Men Formen in the west, with the Men Romen to the south. Though no
more than a road of packed earth, it is clear that travellers have been here
recently, both by the mark of horseshoes and the print of boots. Animal tracks
also cross the trail at intervals and droppings dot the trail. Obviously some
beasts can survive amongst the indigestible vegetation.
name: The Formen-Romen Trail

desc:
The trail leads through a wide flat area avoiding the hills. There's a little
more plant growth here, a few tall bushes growing on a slight rise a few hundred
yards east. To the south, bracken takes a stranglehold over the land. Thus there
is at least some change to the monotony of a grassy terrain.
name: The Formen-Romen Trail

desc:
The wide open expanse stretches for many miles around. It forms part of the
great open wastes of the Lone-lands, only interrupted by the chain of hills to
the north and east. The soil is of little use for growing and so has never been
cultivated: only grasses and hardy shrubs grow in this wilderness. To the north
a small hill breaks the flat view, but it is itself dwarfed by the shadow of
Weathertop to the east. A depression has formed on that mighty hill's western
flank where one could shelter from the wind. Else one must travel through the
wastes.
name: The Flatlands

desc:
Though dwarfed by Weathertop to the east, being atop this hill commands a
reasonable view of the surrounding area as the flint and chalky soil mean no
trees or shrubs grow to obscure the vista.
name: A Hillock

desc:
This is the southern end of the Weather Hills, where the wavy ridge of many
miles falls away before the mighty rise of Weathertop. Though this trail often
reaches many hundreds of feet above the surrounding flat land, it also dives
down into lower passes which break the length of the ridge of hills. Though the
trail is cunningly disguised for most parts, the fortifications which it once
supplied are still evident to the keen observer from afar. While broken and
overgrown, ancient stoneworks remain part of the landscape.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail passes over the plain here, its travellers hidden from prying eyes by
lines of moss-covered stones on either side of it. Peering between the stones,
the traveller can see small hills to the west and south and a larger hill to the
northeast. The greatest hill of them all, Weathertop, rises high above you close
to the southeast.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
Here the trail runs north and south at the western foot of a steep hill. Two
lines of boulders, higher than men, protect travellers from prying eyes. In the
northwest there is a forest, and beyond it another great hill rises in the
distance.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail hugs the eaves of a dark wood. It would be difficult for any, even
birds, to spy those moving upon it. To the east rise the steep, bare slopes of a
great hill, but the trail continues north and south and the bold could venture
into the trees to the west.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail takes cover under the eaves of a dark wood, following its edge west
and south. To the north and the east, great hills rise towards the sky. Mighty
Weathertop rises in the south, still some leagues off.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail runs north and east, following the edge of the thick forest which
grows to the southwest. Beyond the path, steep hillsides rise towards the sky,
bereft of all cover save grass. On them you would be easily spied, but here even
a bird couldn't spot you.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
Here, in a narrow space between the eaves of the dark wood southwest of you, the
tall, steep sides of the great hill of you, and the barren, exposed plain
stretching into the eastern distance, lies a small trail. It hugs the edge of
the forest, taking shelter from unfriendly eyes under its boughs. The old trees
grow thickly but the trail has somehow remained clear.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail runs east-west, hugging the bottom of a steep, rocky slope at the base
of the hill to the north, and following a small, clear space between the
hillside and the dark eves of the thick wood which lies to the south. A small
patch of sky is visible between the hillside and the treetops.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail runs east-west, following the bottom of the hill to the north and
keeping clear of the dark wood to the south. The northward slope is more gentle
here than elsewhere, and you could walk up it onto the shoulder of the hill. The
trail is lined on both sides by great boulders. The moss and lichen on their
sides speaks of how long they have been here. From between them it is obvious
that they were placed here with a purpose, by folk who wished to travel the line
of the hills without being seen.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail curves around north and east as it rounds the corner of the great
hill. A foothill rises to the west. Southwards, flat plains stretch to the
horizon their end marked by a dark line of trees to your southeast. Then you
follow the trail into a small hollow in the plain, and your view of the lands
about is blocked. A few small, thorny bushes grow here as well as the grass and
weeds.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The great hill in the east slopes downwards much more gently here than
elsewhere, and its sides are covered with grass standing several feet tall. The
trail takes advantage of this, climbing a little way up the hillside to follow a
narrow north-south ravine. You could walk up the shoulder of the hill. To your
west the great, flat plains begin, and a foothill rises, its rounded top level
with the trail, to the southwest.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail runs west and south, close beneath the steep banks of two of the
Weather Hills. A pass between them leads up, eastwards, towards the green- grown
stones of an old wall. The slope of the pass is gentle, but its sides are steep
and high. The green plains stretch off towards the southwest and a small
foothill perhaps a league away.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
Here a hidden trail runs under the shoulder of the great hill to the north.
People travelling on it are protected from the vision of travellers on the
plains or inquisitive birds by a line of great boulders which look from the
outside like simply more rocks on this barren, rocky stretch of hillside. The
slope north of you is almost vertical: nothing could climb it, and someone one
the hilltop above would have to come to the cliff-edge and look straight down to
see you. Barren plains stretch into the distance to the west, but southwards a
small hill rises from them a little more than a league away.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
Here the trail runs closely under the steep slope of the Weather Hills. Its
builders placed a line of boulders along the trail where it rises into view of
the plains, and they protect any travellers here from peering eyes. The trail
continues north into a dell between the great hill to your northeast and a
foothills to your northwest. Eastwards it follows the bottom of the hill.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The old trail runs north-south through a desolate valley between one of the
Weather Hills to the east and one of their foothills to the west. Thin grass
grows here, and stones, and weeds, but nothing else. The eight hundred foot
slope to your east is too steep to climb, but at least a watcher on the top of
the hill would have to come to the edge and look straight down to see you. You
could easily climb the much smaller hill to the west.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The hidden valley between the Weather Hills and their foothill turns west-south
here. At one place a faint trail leads to a narrow pass east through the Weather
Hills. You can see fortifications at the top of the pass, although even at this
distance the green of vines on the stone walls shows them to have been long
abandoned. Perhaps this trail was built by the same people who built the
fortress, for the trail would have been an excellent route to move men and
supplies there swiftly and without the foe's knowledge.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail travels east-west, along the bottom of a small valley between a
foothill to the south and the main ridge of the Weather Hills to the north.
There are a few bushes here, as well as the grass and rocks. You cannot see the
tops of the hills because of the walls of the valley, but they also protect you
from being seen by any watchers.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
Here a hidden trail runs under the shoulder of the great hill to the northeast.
People travelling on it are protected from the vision of travellers on the
plains or inquisitive birds by a line of great boulders which look, from the
outside, like a few more rocks on this barren, rocky stretch of hillside. To the
south, great, barren plains recede into the distance, while a small hill rises
to the west. The trail drops into a valley in the east and continues north into
a small dell.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
The trail runs north-south at the foot of the great hill to your east. A line of
great boulders protects travellers on the path from being spied upon by
creatures on the plains to the west or birds in the air above them. The plains
are stony, but support a thin growth of grass and thistles. They stretch to the
western horizon. Another grey-green hill rises to the north.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
You are standing on a wide path, on the top of the hills. But the forest is so
dense, you can't see what really surrounds this forest. You guess the hills must
continue to the north.
name: The High Path

desc:
You are standing on a wide path, on the top of the hills. But the forest is so
dense, you can't see what really surrounds this forest. You guess the hills must
continue to the north.
name: The High Path

desc:
You are standing in a dense forest, just along the road. You can see the forest
continuing to the north, becoming higher and higher. The soil is so wet here
that your shoes are all full of mud. Maybe you should take some time to wash
them up ? You hear the soft sound of falling water just next to you. Time for a
bath ? You look at your dirty equipment : really not presentable !
name: The High Forest

desc:
You are in front of a steep cliff. It is impossible to go further. You notice a
small brook. Some clear water springs out between the rocks; it runs down in an
agreeable melody; you wish you could hear such music more often. The only exit
is south.
name: The Small Waterfall

desc:
You are in a very small cavern; it is so small that you must bend over to enter
it. After your pass, the dense vegetation which recovers the entry closes
itself. You seem to be trapped; in the dark, you notice some living forms. You
try to search for these moving shadows, but your light is really too weak.
name: A Dirty Cavern

desc:
You are in small dark tunnel. You must bend over to advance further and you can
feel the numerous rocks on the ground, scratching your poor legs. This passage
seems very ancient, but well kept.
name: A Small Tunnel

desc:
You are in front of a dark chapel. You can't remember seeing such a strange
architecture. It seems that the chapel was built not so long ago... You notice a
perfect lack of beauty ! Should you really enter it ?
name: Entrance to the Dark Chapel

desc:
You are in a small hall. Some torches seem to be hanging on the side of the
wall; they seem to have been just put off as you came in. You can't see through
the darkness of the corridor; you fumble at nearly every step. Why is this room
so unfriendly ? Obviously, you shouldn't stay here for long...
name: The Dark Hall

desc:
This room was obviously built for one unique reason; they must use this room to
hide themselves from the intruders. Maybe are you considered as an intruder.
Maybe you will be punished in consequence...
name: The Hidden Room

desc:
You've just entered the remains of a burned out hut once belonging to a human or
possibly hobbit occupant. There are blackened orc arrows protruding from the
wall along with other signs of battle. The fire which consumed this place has
long since burned out. You can only make out the outlines of this once beautiful
dwelling.
name: Blackened Hut

desc:
There are some tracks here in the grass, leading to a small wooden lodge south
of here on the plains. The smell of burned and charred wood reaches you from
there. Thick brush among old pine trees growing in the north and east of here
would slow down progress to a halt.
name: The Southern Plains

desc:
This is a very dry, hard trail in the grass with some old footprints cast in it.
The mud is so dry that there are cracks throughout the path. The trail ends
behind a building to the north, and disappears in equally hard and dry land to
the south.
name: Cracked Path

desc:
Although the earth is hard and dry, cracked up in deep fissures in many places,
a few aspen trees manage to survive here north of the road. Birdsong can be
heard from within the grove, and you catch a glimpse of a finch among the leaves
and branches.
name: Cracked Earth

desc:
Deep scars are cut in the dry earth here. Fissures and ravines have opened up
the flatlands as if severe quakes have shook the area. You have no problem
traversing these openings though, as they are only about a yard wide at most.
The constant threat that the edges might collapse and trap you makes progress
slow anyhow, and venturing further east would be very dangerous.
name: Cracked Earth

desc:
The land here is cracked, and the further east you get the larger the fissures
and ravines grow. Wherever you look, cracked land is before your eyes, but north
and west grass grows with a grey tint. Far south you spot the hedge and the old
east road.
name: Cracked Earth

desc:
You are in the only library of the Chapel. A few books are still on a shelf, but
most of them are lying on the big table in the middle of the room. A lantern is
still on the table...somebody must have worked here not so long ago!
name: The Small Library

desc:
You are in a small hall. Some torches seem to be hanging on the side of the
wall; they seem to have been just put off as you came in. You can't see through
the darkness of the corridor; you fumble at nearly every step. Why is this room
so unfriendly ? Obviously, you shouldn't stay here for long...
name: The Dark Hall

desc:
You are in a small hall. Some torches seem to be hanging on the side of the
wall; they seem to have been just put off as you came in. You can't see through
the darkness of the corridor; you fumble at nearly every step. Why is this room
so unfriendly ? Obviously, you shouldn't stay here for long...
name: The Dark Hall

desc:
You are standing in the biggest room of the Dark Chapel. The cult seems to take
place here. You can see a sacrificial altar on your right. You shiver as you see
some mortal remains...
name: The Praying Room

desc:
You are standing on some small hills. You can see that they must have been once
very impressive; they seem to have been partly destroyed. But, you can still
walk easily on them.
name: The Broken Hills

desc:
You are standing on some small hills. You can see that they must have been once
very impressive; they seem to have been partly destroyed. But, you can still
walk easily on them.
name: The Broken Hills

desc:
You find yourself amid a landscape of rolling hills. But where once the
countryside must have been beautiful to behold, now it is blighted and broken.
The devastation has left comparitively little vegetation to hinder travel in
this area, and progress to the north, east and west appears to be easy.
name: The Broken Hills

desc:
You are standing on some small hills. You can see that they must have been once
very impressive; they seem to have been partly destroyed. But, you can still
walk easily on them.
name: The Broken Hills

desc:
You are standing on some small hills. You can see that they must have been once
very impressive; they seem to have been partly destroyed. But, you can still
walk easily on them.
name: The Broken Hills

desc:
You are standing among some small hills. They are covered with thin grass;
bushes grow in the ravines among them. From the tops of the hills, you can see
that the land flattens into a plain to the north, and beyond it and beyond the
small hills to your east a much more impressive line of hills dominates the
horizon.
name: The Broken Hills

desc:
You are standing on the thin grass that covers some small hills. From the tops
of the hills you can see far about you. To the north the land flattens into a
grassland, and beyond that lie the dark eaves of a forest. To the northeast a
range of impressive hills march into the distance. But the most impressive hill
of all, thousand-foot Weathertop, rises to the east. The small hills themselves
continue south.
name: The Broken Hills

desc:
You are near the eastern edge of a vast, desolate plain. The short grass could
cover nothing larger than a bug. You see a thick wood northeast of you. Further
off, a line of great hills looms in a great arc from the north to the flat head
of Weathertop in the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a desolate plain. The short grass could cover nothing larger than a
bug. It stretches off to west and the east but in the north there is a dark wall
of trees. Beyond the wood looms the top of a great hill. Another hill rises in
the east. To the south rise much smaller hills. Beyond them, to the southeast,
you can see the conical top of Weathertop against the sky.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a desolate plain. The short grass could cover nothing larger than a
bug. It stretches off to east and west but in the north there is a dark wall of
trees. Beyond the wood looms the top of a great hill. Another dominates the
eastern horizon. To the south, the plain ends in a terrain of small hills and
valleys. Further off, to the southeast, you can see the conical top of
Weathertop against the sky.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a desolate plain. The short grass could cover nothing larger than a
bug. It stretches off to west but in the north there is a dark wall of trees and
to the east the grassy slopes of a great hill rise steeply. Another treeless
hill looms beyond the wood. To the south, the land rises and falls in much
smaller hills and valleys. Beyond them, to the southeast, you can see the
conical top of Weathertop against the sky.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
An old Human village from many years ago stands across the plains. Its formerly
protective walls are crumbling over time, though still managing, perhaps, to do
their old job to some extent. The only simple entrance to the village is here,
where an old battered gate still hangs on rusted hinges. No sounds can be heard
from within; it's unlikely that anything would want to live in this place.
name: Village Entrance

desc:
You are standing in the lee of the walls of a deserted town. Although untended
for many years, the walls still block your way west and your entry into the
town. About you only short grass and small plants grow, where once there must
have been prosperous fields. These plains stretch into the distance in all
directions. On the horizon a line of tall hills marches from the north to the
southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass.
Far to the east there is a dark line of trees. Even further off loom great
hills, stretching from the distant shape of Weathertop in the southeast into the
distance in the north. To south and west there are only the plains.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass.
In the east there is a dark line of trees. Even further off loom great hills,
stretching from the distant shape of Weathertop in the southeast into the
distance in the north. To south and west there are only the plains.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are at the edge of a vast stretch of desolate plains. To the east, the thin
grasses abruptly give way to large trees standing in a thick forest. To your
north loom the Weather Hills. Over the tops of the trees you can see the flat
head of Weathertop in the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
A grove of gnarled old oak trees, some with trunks four or five yards across,
but still no taller than the other trees, dominate the lesser trees and bushes.
All is silent and still. Unusual herbs and flowers grow here.
name: An Ancient Wood

desc:
You emerge from yet another tangle to find yourself in a small clearing. After
the thick forest, it is strange to see the sky clearly again. All around the
clearing are more bushes, and above them the foliage of the trees.
name: Forest Clearing

desc:
Trees grow thickly above you. Mosses and lichens grow on the trunks of hoary oak
and ancient pine alike. Small plants and fungi grow in their shade. It is
impossible to travel in a straight line, though you try. Only the sounds of the
wind in the trees and of the activity of the forest life disturb the quiet.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
This forest is a tangle of small trees, some ancient, some young, and thick
bushes. Small trails start and stop, without apparent purpose. Butterflies hover
near wildflowers, and you can hear birds singing above.
name: Thick Forest

desc:
With effort, you make your way between the trees. Few are large, but they grow
thickly. From time to time you think you have found a little path, and that
makes travel easier for a little while, but always it ends nowhere.
name: Thick Forest

desc:
You are at the edge of a vast stretch of desolate plains. To the east, the thin
grasses abruptly give way to large trees standing in a thick wood. To your north
loom the Weather Hills, and one of their foothills rises out of the plains to
the northwest. Over the tops of the trees you can see the flat head of
Weathertop in the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. The short grass could cover nothing larger
than a bug. To the north a small hill rises out of the plains. It is as barren
as they. To the east you can see a dark line of trees. Great hills loom in the
distance, forming a great arc from the north to the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. The short grass could cover nothing larger
than a bug. A foothill lies to the northeast, and you can see a dark line of
trees far off to the east. A line of great hills looms in the distance, forming
a great arc from the north to the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones and short thistles stick out
among the thin grass. To your southwest stand the walls of a long-deserted town.
In the distance a line of great hills loom, forming a great arc from the north
to the southeast. In the other directions the plains stretch to the horizon
without interruption.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast plain. Thin grasses, thistles, and other small plants make a
living on the thin soil. A line of great hills looms in the distance, forming a
great arc from the north to the southeast. But there is another feature much
closer: to your southeast stand the walls of a human town. There are no signs of
activity in or near it.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
The plains stretch into the distance in all directions. Mostly they are covered
with grass, but thistles, wildflowers and shoots grow here as well. You hear a
humming buzz, faint in the distance it seems, but it stops as you go forward and
then starts again behind you as you go on. To the southeast a walled town still
stands, the only sign of people on the plains. Much further off loom a line of
great hills. They form a great arc from the north to the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones stick out from the thin grass. A
line of great hills looms in the distance, forming a great arc from the north to
the southeast. In the other directions, there are only the plains, stretching
featurelessly into the distance.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass and
thistles. The ground slopes gradually upwards towards a line of hills in the
eastern distance. A small foothill rises but two leagues north. In the other
directions, there are only flat plains.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are near the northern edge of a vast stretch of desolate plains. The short
grass could cover nothing larger than a bug. The Weather Hills loom in the
distance in a line marching from the north to the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are at the northern edge of a vast stretch of desolate plains. The short
grass could cover nothing larger than a bug. The line of the Weather Hills is
still some leagues to the east, but one of their foothills rises directly to
your north.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You easily walk up the small hill's grassy sides. Looking about, you can see far
and wide. A range of great hills, several times as tall as this one, recedes
into the northern and southeastern distances. The closest of these hills is
barely a league to the east. A forest grows northwest of the hill, and green
plains stretch into the southern distance.
name: Top of a Small Hill

desc:
This flat expanse of ground is dominated by the steep slope of a mighty hill in
the east. To the south, the land rises, towards a much smaller hill, and it
slopes gently westwards, towards a line of alders which marks the end of the
plain. Around you, grasses and small plants grow. The plain continues northward.
name: Narrow Plain

desc:
Here, on a long stretch of flat land perhaps a league wide, the grasses and
thistles of the the plains eke out a living among the stones. The grassland ends
at a line of trees in the west and at the steep rise of a grey-green hill in the
east. It extends south.
name: Narrow Plain

desc:
You are standing on a gentle, grassy slope. It continues to your south, and
slopes gradually westward up to the hilltop, where you can see some overgrown
stone-works. But to the west the hillside falls away steeply towards a great
grassy plain several hundred feet below you. The plains stretch into the
distance. Beyond them the air is hazy.
name: Hillside

desc:
The peak of this hill is covered in stones and thin grass. A long stone wall has
been built along the ridge where the land falls away to both east and west, and
it comes from the south and passes over the hilltop. In many places it has
fallen into grassy piles of stone during the long years since it was last
maintained. The view is spectacular, and you can see far in all directions.
name: Hilltop

desc:
The old wall follows the height of the land from the east and curves northward
up to the top of the hill north of you. In many places it has fallen into mounds
of grassy rubble over the long years, and vines grow on the parts which still
stand. The stony ground about you supports only thin grasses and scattered
wildflowers among the stones, but to your northwest a great shallow hollow in
the centre of the hilltop is filled with bushes and tall grass. The hillside
continues to the east, and only slopes down gently towards the southwest
shoulder of the hill to your west, but in the south it ends abruptly in a great
cliff beyond which grassy plains stretch far into the distance.
name: An Old Stone Wall on a Hillside

desc:
You are standing on a gentle, grassy slope. It continues to your north, and
slopes up gradually to the west, where you can see the ruins of a long stone
wall To the west and to the south the hillside falls away in great cliffs.
Beyond the cliffs lie great green plains. A small foothill interrupts them a
league or so south of you, and in the western distance you can see a dark line
of trees. Beyond that a haze in the air blocks your sight.
name: Hillside

desc:
You have reached the northeast-southwest ridge which forms the backbone of the
Weather Hills. Someone long ago built a dike on its top. The hill slopes gently
down to the west and the north, and a cliff blocks access to the woods that lie
south. But for the dike there are no signs that speaking peoples ever came here,
save perhaps a few places at its base where the grass grows more thickly.
name: At a Grassy Dike

desc:
Short grasses grow here, and there are many rocks of different sizes, but there
are no trees. An earthen dike has been built along the highest part of the hill.
The ground slopes gently downwards from the northeast to the west. It drops off
abruptly to the south, and beyond you can see dark green treetops on the plain
several hundred feet below you. Treeless green plains stretch into the distance
beyond the western slope of the hill.
name: Grassy Hillside

desc:
The hillside is covered with short grasses and wildflowers. It slopes gently
down to the plains to the west and the south. The view is spectacular. Other
great hills rise northwest and southeast of you. Further southeast looms
Weathertop, mightiest of all these hills. Closer, below you, lie a small hill to
the southwest and the dark green treetops of a wood in the south. Beyond them
flat green plains stretch towards the horizon.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You are at the northeast corner of a vast stretch of desolate plains. The short
grass could cover nothing larger than a bug. One of the Weather Hills rises
steeply from the plain little more than a league to the northeast, and much
smaller foothills lie to the east and to the northwest. You can see some old
ruins on the Hill.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
In the lands to the west this would be a landmark, but here it is a mere
foothill before the line of the Weather Hills. Its crown and slopes are treeless
and rocky. To the west and south barren plains recede into the distance, while
north and east stand two great hills.
name: Hill

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass.
To your east looms a line of great hills. You can barely discern ruined
stone-works on their treeless sides. The pass between the nearest two is deep
with steep sides, and in it you can see more ruined fortifications, green-grown
with time. To the north a foothill rises from the plain and to the west and
south there is only the plain, stretching into the distance.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass.
To your east a line of great hills looms. Like the plain they are barren and
treeless. The pass between the nearest two is deep with steep sides. To the
northeast a foothill rises, but to west and south there is only the plain,
stretching into the distance.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast plain. The vegetation is sparse - grass, thistles, and other
weeds - but during the summer it supports many insects and birds. A line of
great hills looms in the distance, forming a great arc from the north to the
southeast. In the other directions, there are only flat plains.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass.
To your east looms a line of great hills. They are barren and treeless. Closer,
small foothills rise from the plain about a league to the north and to the east.
To the west and to the south, the plains stretch on until the sky meets the
grass.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are at the eastern edge of a vast, desolate plain. Not far to the north, the
steep slopes of a huge hill rise towards the sky. The plains continue a little
less than a league eastwards, forming a bay between the northern hill, another
giant in the east, and a much smaller hill which rises gently from the grass to
the southeast. To the west and to the south there are only plains, stretching
away to meet the sky at the horizon.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
The plains here wash up against the hills, forming a small inlet, with great
hills of the main line of the Weather Hills some distance yet to the north and
east and a relatively small foothill just to the south. The bottom of this
valley is bare of any plant larger than the short grass.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You can see far from the top of this small, grassy hill. Great hills rise
steeply to the north, the northeast and far to the east. You can see a small
tower on the top of the northern one. To the southeast a thick wood covers the
ground in darkness. Beyond it, there is another hill in the distance. To the
south, to the west, and to the northwest the green plains stretch on,
featureless.
name: Foothill

desc:
You are in a vast, desolate plain. The short grass could cover nothing larger
than a bug. To the east a small foothill rises out of the sea of grass. A line
of great hills looms in a great arc from the north to the southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, desolate plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass.
A line of great hills looms in the distance, forming a great arc from the north
to the southeast. Closer, but still a league to the east, a smaller hill rises
gently. In the other directions, there are only the plains, stretching
featurelessly into the distance.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast plain. A hobbit might call it desolate, for there are no signs
of inhabitation, and no trees or food-bearing plants. An elf could sit here for
hours with pleasure, watching the patterns of the clouds and the bumblebee
methodically fertilising a small patch of yellow flowers nearby. A line of great
hills looms in the distance, forming a great arc from the north to the
southeast. To the west and to the south there are only the plains, stretching
into the distance.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
This must have once been the village square. On each side are derelict buildings
- houses and shops. The ground was once cobbled, probably creating a smooth but
firm pavement, but time hasn't been kind to it. Here and there stones are
missing, and those that remain are chipped and dirty. On one side of the square,
a building still remains in a relatively good state. A sign hangs from an
outstretching bar, squeaking in the breeze. The worn street continues eastwards.
name: Cobblestone Road

desc:
The name of this inn is now ironically suitable. Although it has stood far
better than any of the other buildings in the village, it still couldn't be said
it was in a good state. Rotting chairs are sprawled on the floor, rusty
candlesticks still holding stubby candles beside them. What still remains
intact, however, are the stairs leading up to the rooms. The rooms themselves
haven't fared so well - only one still remains.
name: Fallen Inn

desc:
You shudder to think what kind of guests would now stay in this room. The roof
has long since fallen in, collapsing on the torn and decaying beds. As you look
over the beds, you're shocked to see beneath some rubble two skeletons large
enough to be Human or Elven. A feeling of death surrounds the room, enough to
place fear into the most evil heart.
name: Guest Room

desc:
Here, the small village ends. This must have been where most of the villagers
lived, as from what can still be made from the buildings, most look like places
of dwelling. Also, a quaint stone well sits tidily in the centre of the road,
likely placed to be most convenient to the inhabitants. None of the buildings
are now safe to enter, and the village wall prevents any exit other than back
west.
name: End of Road

desc:
The land dips here, forming a little bowl on the top of the hill. The grasses
grow taller here, and bushes stand out among them. Insects hum and chirp and
buzz and birds sing. Some large animal has been digging among the ground. But
there is still no surface water here.
name: Hilltop Depression

desc:
This hill's top forms a shallow bowl several miles across. You are standing on
the bowl's southern rim. The rim is fairly flat, sloping down gently towards the
hollow northwards and towards the shoulder of the hill in the east, and
continuing westwards. To the south the hill slopes very steeply down into a
narrow valley, beyond which another hill rises. An old stone wall is built along
the top of the rim, running west-east. Vines grow on those parts of it which
have not collapsed.
name: An Old Stone Wall on a Hillside

desc:
The broad hillside is fairly flat. It slopes gently upwards to south and east.
You can see another hilltop across a deep but narrow valley which lies north of
here. In the west the hill ends abruptly.
name: Grassy Hillside

desc:
A large wall once blocked passage from the East here. Now many of its stones
form grassy heaps on the hillside, and what yet stands could be climbed over in
many places. The hillside slopes gently up to the wall. It ends at a rocky
height in the southeast and follows the ridge down into the pass north of you.
The pass is narrow and another hilltop is almost level with you on the other
side. A tower has been built at the top of the height.
name: At an Old Wall

desc:
You have come to a grassy field which seems to be at the top of the world. The
ground falls steeply away from you in three directions. To the north, a steep
climb leads down to a deep valley between this hill and the one north of it. In
the west the hill ends at a great cliff. Grassy plains far below you stretch
into a blue distance. To the south, the field slopes more gently down towards
the hill's southwestern shoulder. Only to the east does the level field
continue.
name: Hilltop

desc:
The river at this point is crisp and rather clear. It is still dark and green
and shapes can be seen moving along in the waters. They look just like twigs or
something but advice says not to just jump right into the waters. You can float
down the river and just relax or paddle up it and see what is at its end. Right
now though you can not help but sip the cool clear green water of this river. It
refreshes you from your long days journey.
name: The Green River

desc:
The river is quite clear here. The rocks in the water along for a slightly
swifter current than what looks like is headed down south. Headed north though
the river doesn't look as if it is as peaceful and calm. Best here is just to
get out and wade or swim through the river heading up north. The river looks to
be turning more into a creek than a river as you head north. Then again with the
sun moving down. You could be mistaken. In either case, right now you feel like
you want to just take a nap with the sounds of the rushing water all around you.
name: A Green River

desc:
All the walls in this room are covered with bookshelves, except in the north-
west where a clean fireplace occupies the corner, and on the southern wall is a
painting resembling an old man sternly watching what goes on in this room. An
inscription runs all along the top of the walls, just below the ceiling. The
only furniture in this room is a pleasant armchair with a small table standing
in the middle of the room upon the red, strangely patterned floor.
name: A Red Library

desc:
This hall is fairly empty and featureless. A blue shorthaired carpet covers the
dark parquet floor, but no furniture except a pedestal with an empty pot has
been placed here. All doors exiting this room has been barred shut with many
thick planks, except for one leading north through an archway. Above the stairs
leading down south hangs a dramatic painting on the wall, depicting a
battlefield.
name: Upper Hall

desc:
This is a narrow path which hugs the contour of the final hill of the chain of
hills which are the Emyn Sul. The trail winds its way into the hills west from
here, but cunningly it is disguised and also obscures any travellers. However on
the further part of the trail south, climbers are not so fortunate. As the trail
ascends the north-west slope of Weathertop, anyone becomes quite conspicuous,
visible for miles around.
name: The Trail to Weathertop

desc:
Wandering among the Weather Hills is fairly easy, as long as there is a path to
follow, but here that is not the case. The surrounding area is so lush with
grass and undergrowth that even a poorly-maintained trail would be a relief to
the weary traveller.
name: Southern End of the Weather Hills

desc:
This is the western end of the valley, the ground rising in every direction bar
the east. This place seems to act as a refuge for much of the wildlife in the
area, due both to the shelter the valley gives, and to the spring on the
southern slope. A stream trickles down into the valley floor.
name: Valley's End

desc:
There is a great view of the Emyn Sul stretching off northwest for many leagues.
Except for the valley below, most of the countryside is barren and inhospitable.
Down on the eastern slopes a pool of water and a gully lie.
name: Northern Flank of Weathertop

desc:
On the top of the great hill one sees the scant remains of the Tower of Amon
Sul, the greatest fortress in Eriador in the Third Age. It was destroyed in the
wars between the Dunedain of the North and the armies of Angmar, and little
survives to this day. Marking the outer wall of the fortress, a wide ring of
ancient stone-work stands, crumbled by the centuries or covered with age-long
grass. By standing upon this ring, one is afforded an unparalleled view of the
lands around. Within the centre of the circle, a cairn of broken stones is
piled, a memorial to the lost by someone now long forgotten.
name: The Crown of Weathertop

desc:
The wind is harsh and forgiving on the slopes of Amon Sul and with such poor
soil, there are no trees to offer shelter. As one seeks to maintain a purchase
on the hill, the grasses whip around ones legs while the gorse and heather snag
and tear. The only reward is that the vista of many lands is laid out. An even
better view of these lands can be got by climbing to the top of the hill.
name: Southern Flank of Weathertop

desc:
The path cuts through the choking bracken, passing before the foot of Amon Sul,
the conical shaped hill to the north. It was there, an age ago, that Elendil
waited for a sign of the approach of Gil-galad's army. They marched into the
east, many never to return this way again.
name: The Formen-Romen Trail

desc:
The wilderness is no longer so flat here as the ground begins to gently slope
upwards onto the lower flanks of Amon Sul-the last and greatest of the Emyn Sul.
A trail to the south-west crosses the bracken-covered plains, showing the way to
the traveller who wants to cross the open land.
name: The Foot of Weathertop

desc:
This hollow sits sheltered on the western flank of Weathertop, where the slope
of the hill becomes too steep to climb. Rocks that have fallen from higher up
the hill, have at some stage been organised into a low wall by visitors to this
place. From their shape it is clear that many were originally cut for a
building, though no structure remains. The other sides of the dell slope up
pleasantly, preventing the worst of the winds but allowing refreshing breezes.
Beyond the grassy sides to the hollow lies the monotony of the flatlands which
encircle Weathertop and the Weather Hills. As a result of this depression, water
which soaks through Weathertop's porous rock comes to the surface.
name: A Secluded Dell

desc:
This path was built to supply the fortress which once stood on the top of Amon
Sul-the highest point for many leagues. Climbing up from the lower slopes of the
hill to the north-west, it winds its way slowly up the harsh ascent of the great
hill. No trees or thick vegetation grow to hide one from spying eyes.
name: The Trail to Weathertop

desc:
This final slope to the summit of the hill is steep and rocky, drawing heavy
breath from any climber up the trail. The trail winds its way down the slope,
first moving west, then north, as it tries to minimise the gradient. To leave
this path would invite more toil, whether going up or down. However, it is
possible to manoeuvre across the west- or north-facing sides of the hill if one
desires.
name: The Trail to Weathertop

desc:
Hard-work indeed, this flank of Amon Sul is both extremely steep and plagued by
high winds. If one wants to climb further up, there are no easy means to except
by striking out for the trail to the north or expending considerable energy and
climbing directly up to the summit. The grass waves across the slope creating a
featureless yellow-green sea of vegetation, while islands of heather break up
the scenery.
name: Western Flank of Weathertop

desc:
This is part of the southwest side of conical Weathertop. By the standards of
the surrounding plains, the soil here is quite fertile, sustaining a variety of
short grasses and a few hardy flowers, but mainly gorse and heather. The
elevation is sufficient to see many of the surrounding lands, but to gain an
unparalleled vantage point only the very top of the hill will suffice.
name: Heathery Slope

desc:
Here, in a small hollow, protected from the elements is a small shelter. The
walls are merely the baked earth from which the shelter has been cut, but in
some places pelts have been nailed up to provide a better backdrop. There seems
to be adequate space and facilities for several men to survive: a water bowl and
jug, a pile of kindling, some hares hanging from rafters to smoke, and some
pallets for sleeping. A small chimney dug into the earth provides ventilation.
name: Under the Dike

desc:
Along this stretch of the northern border of the old kingdom of Cardolan, the
great hedge which marked the divide is broken. The terrain this side of the East
Road is flat and generally covered with grass. However, the carpeting is uneven
as the soil deteriorates in quality, compared to the verdant green of the downs.
The trail that shadows the southern side of the hedge, is joined by a wide path
from the north which comes through the breach from the East Road a few miles
distant.
name: A Breach in the Hedge

desc:
Though there is no great hill to avoid or contour to follow, nevertheless the
hedge and its attendant trail meanders here, producing a mild kink in the long
thorny chain. The track is particularly obscure here as travellers have taken
other routes across the plains. Little life disturbs the peace, except for a few
birds who fly in and out of the briars.
name: Bend in the Hedge-trail

desc:
West of the Emyn Sul, though the ground is not very fertile the area surrounding
one is carpeted in green. Verdant bracken has taken a hold here, smothering the
land in its choking grasp.
name: The Bracken-covered Land

desc:
This is a narrow watery tunnel which descends to the east from where one can see
the tunnel opening out. From the west, the waters froth and foam as they tumble
along the tunnel, preventing one climbing up that way. Though this watery stream
seems natural enough, the metal grating passing above is certainly made to
dispose of things into the waters.
name: The Watery Chute

desc:
This is the point at which the waters of the hill emerge from the rock, forming
a pool in this gully on the side of Weathertop. A few stunted poplars grow on
the rocky sides of the gully where the waters splash. The water then flows over
the limestone, following an ages-old watercourse down the slope and out into the
endless plains of the En Eredoriath. Disturbingly, the pool show signs that
someone has polluted the source, as a crimson-coloured foam floats on the edge.
name: The Source of the Nen-i-Sul

desc:
Nestled at the foot of the northern flank of Weathertop, and southeast of the
Weather hills, lies this valley. The slope is quite gentle, as the valley leaves
the shelter of the hills. The vegetation supported is varied and substantial
compared to the plains north and east. Perhaps the drainage provided by the
valley is the clue to this.
name: Mouth of the Valley

desc:
This is the eastward side of Weathertop, its conical flank supporting no more
than hardy grasses to mar its countenance. The flinty soil is hard and porous
and rainwater quickly disappears into the ground, only collecting in ravines.
There hardy shrubs have a chance of surviving. Such a place is on the northeast
face of the hill, from where one hears the babble of water.
name: Eastern Flank of Weathertop

desc:
With the constant strong winds and the flinty soil, life here is harsh. On the
exposed climbs of the hill, grass grows amongst the wide swathes of russet
heather. Within the dips and runs cut by small streams, small flowering plants
and short bushes also prosper, while there is evidence of rabbit burrows.
name: Grassy Slope

desc:
From here on east, the Lone-lands proper start: the En Eredoriath. The soil
and/or climate must change here in the vicinity of Amon Sul. Bracken which
chokes the land for many miles in the west ends. At its ragged border, grasses
and irregular stones form the landscape. Though flat, the surface is rugged
enough to deter extended travel, particularly with the proximity of the East
Road to the south and east.
name: Border of the Lone-lands

desc:
The river bends, changing from a southerly course to a more easterly one. Along
the edge of the waters, stands of willows and small poplars derive sustenance
from what is otherwise an inhospitable land. The high sides to the ravine make
climbing out of the watercourse very hard.
name: The Nen-i-Sul

desc:
Unlike its course upstream, the waters of the Nen-i-Sul can now only be crossed
using a craft or by swimming. The river has cut a gully in the limestone of the
plains making it impossible to climb onto the plains east.
name: The Nen-i-Sul

desc:
The water runs down this gully cut into the eastern flank of Weathertop. Only at
the edge of the watercourse is there any significant vegetation. Elsewhere on
the slope only short, sturdy grasses grow in the harsh soil. The Nen-i-Sul at
this point is only an average stream, easily fordable.
name: The Nen-i-Sul

desc:
The landscape is scarred by erosion and the plants struggle to grow as best they
can. With no trees or large bushes, the wind blows without respite, blowing dust
across the terrain. There are no pools or dips which harbour water-everywhere is
dry. With this kind of terrain it is obvious why these plains have never been
populated. The Lone-lands indeed!
name: The Sparse Land

desc:
The Nen-i-Sul flows eastward, cutting this steep ravine in the limestone bedrock
of the plains. The high bluffs on either side of the river prevent one climbing
out to the plains some dozen yards above. Gullies along the side sometimes
contain small streams which trickle down to the watercourse, swelling the river
with every mile.
name: The Nen-i-Sul

desc:
In the course of its route to the sea, the Nen-i-Sul, known to men as the
Weatherwater, turns this way and that. This bend takes the river again south.
name: Bend in the Nen-i-Sul

desc:
The empty lands lie all about, though the presence of the ravine a mile or so to
the south means, one can only gaze beyond in that direction. This is a dull and
inauspicious land, broken by very few major landmarks since the beginning of
time. If the hill of Weathertop had been a part of a mountain chain like those
in the far east, it would be a very minor summit. In this land, it is a
dominating influence on the landscape, its conical shape renowned as a marker by
which travellers may guide themselves.
name: North of the Ravine

desc:
Apart from the course of the Nen-i-Sul to the west, little water lies on the
land. Therefore the cracked surface is harsh on vegetation, only those plants
adapted to an arid climate managing to flourish to any degree. From the cropping
of the grass, animals must also live here but probably most are only small, such
as like rodents or hares.
name: The Sparse Land

desc:
The land about is certainly flat, and the thin soil suggests the name. Animal
tracks are few but still evident on the ground. These marks range from small paw
to large hoof. The odour of mammals drift on the wind to ones nostrils, yet
though the brush hardly limits one's view, it is hard to catch a glimpse of the
lands inhabitants.
name: The Sparse Land

desc:
Northeast of Weathertop and into the vast reaches of the Oiolad, the plains are
flat as far as the eye can see. Closer inspection reveals the eroded nature of
the terrain. Short tufts of grass spring from the unlikeliest of places: cracks
and crevices or a patch of remaining soil. Even weeds find it hard to grow as
the ground dries quickly after the rain, the drops disappearing into the earth.
name: The Sparse Land

desc:
This is the East Road, raised above the surrounding barren lands which form the
western limits of the Lone-lands. No signs of life for miles around make the
name quite apt, though the locale is not without features. Barring the way
south-east is the thorny wall of hedge that marked the ancient border of
Cardolan, one of the divided kingdoms of Arnor. Meanwhile further away, to the
north-west is the conical shape of Weathertop, the last of the Weather Hills.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
The road through the Lone-lands continues on its east-west route. North lies the
great expanse of plains, scrub and stunted shrub dotting the landscape.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
The road bends before continuing along its seemingly endless voyage across
Eriador. The landscape is dominated by the terrain to the northwest, which
consists of a range of hills rising to almost a thousand feet in height. The
largest is also the nearest and it is the well-known Weathertop.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
The mighty road, pitted by pot holes and tumbled stones, stands raised on its
bank above the surroundings. This point near the southern tip of the last of the
hills marks the border between the lands west, and the Lone-lands east. Only the
hedge of bushes spoils the nearby view, its thick thorns allowing one only a
glimpse of the lands to the south.
name: The Old East Road

desc:
Near the border of the Lone-lands with the more hospitable South Downs, the
hedge grows as far as the eye can see to the east and to the west. The trail
which follows the hedge here splits, one route leading south into the plains.
name: A Split in the Trail

desc:
Long ago this trail within the northern border of Cardolan was probably busy
with traffic, taking as it does a reasonably straight route beside the
impenetrable briars of the Great Hedge. These days it is a barely traceable path
among the short tufts of grass which grow in the thin and rocky soil.
name: The Hedge-trail

desc:
The trail bends, though whether this bend is to follow the changed course of the
great hedge, or the hedge was cut to keep to the side of the track is only of
academic interest. The reason for the deviation of the trail lies east a few
miles away in the plains. A deep cleave in the barren land cuts across the
route, its path going from north-west to south-east.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
The trail beside the impenetrable briars of the hedge stands at the top of a
steep descent. Even here with the narrow gully, the cultivators of the hedge
have ensured continuity, barring the way north.
name: Top of the Trail

desc:
At this point in the ravine, the river has been artificially widened. Rocks have
been laid across the deeper stretches so that it manages no more than a couple
of feet in depth, shallow enough even for a brave or adventurous hobbit. Plants
makes good use of this sheltered environment, sedges growing in the shallows
while willows stand on the shores. Birds are also evident, particularly mallards
which are unknown in any other part of the Lone-lands. The path that crosses the
ford climbs back out of the valley on both sides.
name: Ford over the Nen-i-Sul

desc:
The course of the ravine cut by the river bends again, flowing east. At this
turning, several small watercourses tumble down into the canyon from the parched
land around. Though the change in course has widened the bend through erosion,
there is no practical way to climb over the limestone bluffs.
name: Bend in the Nen-i-Sul

desc:
The East Road crosses the Nen-i-Sul river. Since the river flows at the bottom
of a sizeable ravine, an impressive bridge spans the sides in a single arch of
twenty yards. The sides of the bridge are walled by the same limestone of the
land, though with the centuries the walls have become badly eroded. The
underside of the bridge acts as an amplifier for sounds from below, so the
echoes of splashing reverberate, as the river passes below.
name: Bridge over the Nen-i-Sul

desc:
The Lone-lands of Eriador. Few have never heard of them, as they cover most of
the country. However, few dare to visit them, as they are an unforgiving for
those used to the comforts of home. Small settlements such as Bree and the Shire
exist like islands upon a sea of rolling plains and wastes. Many years ago men
from the west might have been found here. Since the Kingdom in the North fell to
Angmar, this area has died with it.
name: Plains

desc:
From Bree to Rivendell, the plains of Eriador are infamous. Stretching across
the land like a dull blanket, they dutifully stretch onwards. No water sources
mean few plants and few animals, though the place is by no means a desert.
Barren is the most apt description.
name: Plains

desc:
This stretch of the Lone-lands is especially bleak. The landscape is flat as far
as the eye can see for many miles, yet the terrain is not featureless. Narrow
cracks and wide gullies cross the land where the rainwater has flowed into the
ground. A large depression to the south perhaps has a similar origin.
name: The Cracked Land

desc:
When the North Kingdom of the Men was destroyed, the people of Middle-earth no
longer came to the centre of Eriador as they used to. Most of the areas became
desolate and lifeless. So it was that the legend of the Lone-lands began.
name: Plains

desc:
A shallow canyon has formed here, more than a mile across. It is fairly flat
inside with just the hint of curvature to the depression. However, the sides are
quite steep and pitted with clefts. The vegetation is especially barren, with
not even grass growing. Some dark green weeds sprout in places, breaking from
the hard dirt. The wind swirling around the bowl gives an eerie howl. Even more
disturbing is that the ground seems to be perpetually trembling. It is ever so
slight, but it is constant, like an earthquake about to spring to life. In every
direction, the ways out of this canyon look equally poor, but a brief scramble
should suffice.
name: The Haunting-bowl

desc:
The great road bends, though why the builders shifted the course of the road in
not known. The ever-attendant hedgewall to the south keeps its course so now the
road diverges from the briars by a few miles. This allows some leeway to leave
the road to the east but the barren land is not too encouraging.
name: Bend in the Old East Road

desc:
The road bends in its route. The road to Bree and the Shire now turns south
temporarily before resuming its westward course. Beside the road stands a
disused cistern, once used when the road was young. Beyond the road, the empty
plains of the Lone-lands stretch, marred only by the ravine to the west.
name: Bend in the Old East Road

desc:
The hedge and its attendant trail, descends west into a ravine cut by the flow
of the Nen-i-Sul. Though the descent is not steep, the moisture of the area has
made the ground slippery. Thankfully through lack of use, grass has grown over
much of the path providing traction for the rare traveller.
name: The Hedge-trail

desc:
After bending east, the Nen-i-Sul now turns south again, flowing along this
limestone ravine towards the Hoarwell. There are no shore to the river and the
sides of the ravine offer no purchase, except to small poplars whose roots have
split the rock. Splashed by the water, the lower walls of the canyon are
treacherously coated in mosses of a variety of hue: green, brown and yellow
being common. Black water-lice move amongst this mossy world, while a lone snail
crawls along the razor-sharp edge of a rock, oblivious to the danger.
name: Bend in the Nen-i-Sul

desc:
At this point, the river flows under a bridge which crosses a narrow point in
the ravine in a single span. On the east bank of the river, the poplars grow
about a steep set of stairs cut into the stone, leading up to a side of the
bridge. Within the shadow of the bridge, plants that prefer the shade thrive.
name: Under the Nen-i-Sul Bridge

desc:
This is a dense forest, just along the road. You can see the forest continuing
to the north, becoming higher and higher. You hear the soft sound of falling
water just next to you.
name: The High Forest

desc:
This hill is crowned with a great field. The grass grows thickly here. The field
falls gently away east and west from a north-south ridge on which a grass-grown
dike has been built. The field continues west, and descends into rougher, more
rocky terrain to the east and south. In the north a path along the ridge leads
down into a narrow valley between this hill and the one north of it.
name: Hilltop

desc:
You find a small hollow in the hillside here. A spring bubbles in it, and a
brook flows from it down the hillside towards the eastern plains. Someone has
taken advantage of this water source to build a campsite: there is a firepit,
and a stack of firewood, and the hollow itself provides shelter from the winds.
There is a pass beyond the steep slope to the north, and beyond it are the
eastern slopes of another great hill.
name: Sheltered Dell

desc:
The hillside slopes gently down towards plains in the east, and up towards the
top of the hill in the northwest. It is covered with rocks, short grasses, and
thistles. An old stone wall snakes up from the valley in the south and turns
west towards the hilltop. Many of its stones have fallen loose, and in places it
is little more than a tall, grassy pile of stone. Beyond the valley the top of
another hill rises above you.
name: Hillside

desc:
The pass forms a deep, narrow valley between the steep sides of two of the
Weather Hills. It slopes gently up eastwards towards the top of the pass, which
is blocked by a ruined fortress.
name: Pass through the Weather Hills

desc:
Long ago someone built a fortress here, and you can see that that would be an
excellent place to block east-west travel if you were a King and so minded. But
now ivy grows on the fortress' walls, and low bushes bloom before its walls,
their yellow flowers splashed blood-red as if the land itself remembers the
soldiers who fought and died here. Where its gate once stood there is now only a
gaping hole. The pass itself slopes gently down towards the plains in the west.
The ridge that forms the backbone of the hills slopes gently up the northern
hill, and steeply up the southern one, and a wall has been built atop it where
the slope of the land alone is not enough to block travel.
name: At the Top of the Pass

desc:
A long time ago a fortress was built here, where it would block passage through
the Weather Hills. Although much damage has been done to the fortress since it
was last inhabited, from the great breach in the eastern wall and the ruin of
the west gate to the slow work of wind, rain and vine on the stones of the
walls, the craftsmanship is still plainly recognisable as that of the Dunedain,
the humans who dwelt here long ago in friendship with the Elves of Rivendell and
the Grey Havens. But now it is at peace, and the only sound is the whisper of
the wind.
name: Old Fortress Ruins

desc:
Once, men must have been able to see far and wide from the battlements of this
tower, but the wooden stairs and roof have long rotted away, leaving only the
stone walls to grow ivy and the stone floor open to the Sun. Nevertheless, the
tower is in better condition than the other fortifications among these hills. It
appears that it was abandoned, not captured.
name: An Abandoned Watchtower

desc:
You are just south of the top of the rocky crag which forms the summit of this
hill. A league beyond it you can see another hill. To the west you look over the
wide grassy shoulder of the hill to see the plains stretching into the blue
distance. The rock of the crag continues eastwards, forming a steep-sided ridge
which leads down to the pass below you. Beyond it you see plains far below you,
and in the far distance there is perhaps a glint of white. You can see the top
of a slightly smaller hill a league south of you, but the only way to it is by
the ridge, for there is a steep cliff immediately to the south. At the top of
the crag there is an old, abandoned tower. The former location of the door is
marked by a hole in the wall.
name: Before an Abandoned Tower

desc:
The hill's flat shoulder forms a grassy plateau. In the east, a rocky crag forms
the hill's summit. A tower has been built upon it. The plateau stretches some
distance north, where an old stone wall still stands, and west. To the south the
hill ends sharply. There are other hills to the southeast and the northwest.
name: Hilltop Plateau

desc:
The flat shoulder of the hill forms a grassy plateau, which stretches some
distance to the east before ending abruptly in a rocky crag. An old tower stands
atop the peak of the crag, looking down on you imperiously. The plateau slopes
gently to the north, but falls off abruptly to the south and west, offering a
spectacular view of the plains stretching into the distance below you.
name: Hilltop Plateau

desc:
Not even thin grass grows on this ridge of solid rock. It runs northwest-
southeast, and the sides fall off steeply. To the southeast it slopes gently
down to the pass; to the northwest it rises to a rocky crag, on which someone
long ago built and abandoned a small tower. The view to east and west is
spectacular, but the unobstructed wind blows fiercely.
name: On a Ridge

desc:
Here the pass was once blocked by a stone wall. The workmanship is similar to
that of Fornost. But long ago it was breached by some assault, and since then
only the wind and the rain and the grass have worked on it. It would be easy to
climb through the ruins at the old breach. The hillside north of you is steep,
but climbable. A ridge of black rock runs up it, ending in a crag far above you.
The side of the southern hill goes upward more gently, and westward the pass
slopes down towards the plain.
name: A Ruined Wall

desc:
The pass runs east-west under the shoulders of two great hills. It is much
narrower than the slopes about it are tall. Thin grasses cover some of the stony
ground. The northern hill is unclimbable here, for its steep lower slope ends at
a rocky cliff reaching up to the peak. The southern slope is less forbidding,
but still a long, steep climb. The pass continues upwards to the east, where a
stone wall blocks your view. To the west, it slopes gently down to the plains
which stretch off into the horizon.
name: Cleft in the Weather Hills

desc:
Upon descending the rotting steps to the cellar, clouds of putrid air fill your
nostrils. Smells of both decay and mustiness are here, all making your fight
against vomiting difficult to say the least. As you entered, many of the new
inhabitants of the Fallen Inn were stirred from their normal business. High
pitched squeaks and the shuffling of small feet fill the room, adding to your
disgust.
name: Cellar of the Fallen Inn

desc:
You are in a small dark tunnel. To move further, you bend over. As you squeeze
forward, numerous rocks on the ground scratch your poor legs. This passage seems
ancient but well kept. The tunnel ends abruptly in the north.
name: A Small Tunnel

desc:
You are standing on a wide path, on the top of the hills. But the forest is so
dense, you can't see what really surrounds this forest. You guess the hills must
continue to the north.
name: The High Path

desc:
You are standing on a wide path, on the top of the hills. But the forest is so
dense, you can't see what really surrounds this forest. You guess the hills must
continue to the north.
name: The High Path

desc:
Amongst the choking bracken this dirt trail runs. A couple of miles to the south
one can see one of its destination points: the East Road from Bree to Imladris.
Where this trail connects to the road is unclear, though it must lie a league or
two to the east, under the shadow of Weathertop.
name: The Formen-Romen Trail

desc:
Cutting through the expanse of light-green bracken, this trail makes diagonally
from the Men Romen to the south, to the Fornost road in the north-west. This
trail is used by both travellers through the lands and the animals who inhabit
this green sea. With the proximity of Weathertop, the wind continues unabated,
carrying the sappy odour of the bracken.
name: The Formen-Romen Trail

desc:
This part of the plain is as barren as the rest. Only thin grasses grow here,
and there are even more rocks than further south. On the second glance, you
notice a trail. It runs close under the rocky hillside, and is protected from
sight from the plains by strategic dips and rocks. The hill-side is too steep
for anyone on the hilltop to easily see people on the trail at its foot. The
trail follows the hillside north-south.
name: Hidden Trail

desc:
You are in a vast, desolate plain. The short grass and small stones could cover
nothing larger than a bug. In the northeast there is a dark line of trees. A
line of great hills looms in the distance in a great arc from the north to the
southeast. To south and west there are only the plains.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are in a vast, desolate plain. The short grass and small stones could cover
nothing larger than a bug. Far off to the northeast there is a dark line of
trees. A line of great hills looms in the distance in a great arc from the north
to the southeast. To the south and west there are only the plains.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are on a vast, deserted plain. Small stones stick out among the thin grass.
Perhaps a league to the northwest stands a walled town, but no smoke rises from
it to tell of hearth fires, nor are there any signs of people or cultivation on
the plain near it. The only other feature to break the flat plains is a line of
great hills looming on the horizon. They form a great arc from the north to the
southeast.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
You are standing in a dense forest, just along the road. You can see the forest
continuing to the north, becoming higher and higher. The soil is so wet here
that your shoes are all full of mud. Maybe you should take some time to wash
them up ? You hear the soft sound of falling water just next to you. Time for a
bath ? You look at your dirty equipment : really not presentable !
name: The High Forest
